title: STANDARD FOR PAPAYA CXS 183-1993 Adopted in 1993. Revised in 2001. Amended in 2005, 2011.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR PAPAYA CXS 183-1993 Adopted in 1993. Revised in 2001. Amended in 2005, 2011.
sections.summary: Document title and history: 'Standard for Papaya CXS 183-1993', adopted 1993, revised 2001, and amended in 2005 and 2011.

Specifies that the Standard applies to fruits of commercial varieties of papaya (Carica papaya L.) supplied fresh to the consumer after preparation and packaging and excludes papayas intended for industrial processing.

Sets minimum quality requirements (e.g., whole, sound, clean, practically free of pest damage, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign smell/taste, firm, fresh, peduncle ≤1 cm, appropriate development and ripeness to withstand transport) and establishes three classes: 'Extra' (superior, virtually defect‑free), Class I (good quality with slight defects allowed not affecting pulp, skin defects ≤10% surface), and Class II (produce meeting minimums but not higher classes with permitted defects including shape/colour and skin defects up to 15% and slight pest marks), with defects in all classes not allowed to affect the pulp.

Lists minimum requirements for all classes: papayas must be whole, sound (no rotting), clean, practically free of pest damage, practically free of pests affecting appearance, free of abnormal external moisture, free of foreign smell/taste, firm, fresh in appearance, free of temperature damage, with peduncle not exceeding 1 cm, and sufficiently developed/ripened to withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition.

sections.section: 2.1.1 The papayas must have reached an appropriate degree of development and ripeness account being taken of the characteristics of the variety and/or commercial type and the area in which they are grown.
sections.summary: Requires papayas to have reached an appropriate degree of development and ripeness given variety and growing area and to be in condition to withstand transport and handling and to arrive in satisfactory condition at destination.

Classifies papayas into three classes—'Extra', Class I and Class II—with distinct quality levels and permitted defects: Extra (superior, essentially defect‑free), Class I (good quality with slight defects allowed within limits not affecting pulp), and Class II (meets minimum requirements but may show more pronounced defects), and in all cases defects must not affect the pulp.

'Extra' Class: papayas of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type and free of defects except very slight superficial imperfections that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I: papayas of good quality characteristic of the variety with slight defects allowed (e.g., shape defects or skin defects such as mechanical bruising, sun spots or latex burns) provided they do not affect the general appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation and skin defects do not exceed 10% of total surface; smells caused by compliant conservation agents are permitted per footnote.

Class II: papayas that do not qualify for higher classes but meet minimum requirements, where defects in shape, colouring, skin (mechanical bruising, sun spots, latex burns up to 15% of surface) and slight pest marks are allowed provided the pulp is not affected.

Size is determined by fruit weight with a 200 g minimum and size codes A–J corresponding to specified weight ranges (A 200–300 g, B 301–400 g, C 401–500 g, D 501–600 g, E 601–700 g, F 701–800 g, G 801–1100 g, H 1101–1500 g, I 1501–2000 g, J >2001 g).

Allows tolerances by package/lot for produce not meeting class/size requirements: quality tolerances vary by class (see 4.1), and size tolerances permit 10% by number or weight of papayas corresponding to the adjacent size (above/below) with a minimum of 190 g for those in the smallest size range.

Defines quality tolerances by class: 'Extra' allows 5% by number/weight not meeting Extra but meeting Class I or its tolerances; Class I allows 10% not meeting Class I but meeting Class II or its tolerances; Class II allows 10% that satisfy neither the class nor the minimum requirements, excluding produce affected by rotting or otherwise unfit for consumption.

'Extra' Class tolerance: up to 5% by number or weight of papayas in a package/lot may fail to meet 'Extra' requirements provided they meet Class I requirements or, exceptionally, fall within that class's tolerances.

Class I tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of papayas in a package/lot may fail to meet Class I requirements provided they meet Class II requirements or, exceptionally, fall within that class's tolerances.

Class II tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of papayas in a package/lot may neither meet the Class II requirements nor the minimum requirements, except that produce affected by rotting or otherwise unfit for consumption is excluded.

Size tolerances: for all classes up to 10% by number or weight of papayas in a package may be of the immediately larger and/or smaller size than indicated, with a minimum weight of 190 g for papayas packed in the smallest size range.

Provisions concerning presentation: sets uniformity requirements (packages/lots must contain papayas of the same origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size; 'Extra' class requires uniform colour and ripeness; visible contents must represent the whole) and requires packaging and containers that protect produce, use new/clean/food-grade materials and non-toxic printing, comply with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44-1995), and be free of foreign matter and odours.

Uniformity: each package or bulk lot must be uniform—containing papayas of the same origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size—with 'Extra' class also requiring uniform colour and ripeness, and the visible portion must represent the entire contents.

Packaging: papayas must be packed to protect them, using new/clean materials of suitable quality (including recycled food-grade material) and non-toxic inks/adhesives, packed per CXC 44-1995, and containers must meet hygiene, ventilation and resistance requirements and be free of foreign matter and smells.

Description of containers: containers must have appropriate quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation of papayas, and packages/lots must be free of foreign matter and odour.

Marking or labelling: consumer and non-retail packages must meet the General Standard for Labelling plus specific provisions—if produce is not visible the name must be shown; non-retail packages (or accompanying documents for bulk) must legibly and indelibly show identification (exporter/packer/dispatcher and optional code), nature and origin of produce, commercial identification (class, size or average weight, optional unit count and net weight), with an optional official inspection mark and related notes on recycled materials and code-mark labelling.

Consumer packages: in addition to the General Standard for Labelling (CXS 1-1985), if the produce is not visible the package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may include variety/commercial type.
Nature of produce (consumer packages): where produce is not externally visible, each consumer package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may indicate variety or commercial type.

Non-retail containers: each package or accompanying document for bulk shipments must legibly and indelibly show identification, nature and origin of produce, commercial identification (class, size or average weight, optional unit count and net weight), and may bear an official inspection mark, with additional notes on recycled food-grade materials and code-mark labelling.
Identification: non-retail packages must show the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher, with an optional identification code.
Nature of produce (non-retail): the package (or accompanying document if contents are not visible) must state the name of the produce and the variety and/or commercial type.
Origin of produce: packages or accompanying documents must declare the country of origin and may optionally include the district or national/regional/local place name where grown.
Commercial identification: non-retail labelling must indicate class and size (size code or average weight in grams) and may optionally show number of units and net weight.

Official inspection mark (optional): an official inspection mark may be used; recycled material is acceptable if food-grade, and where a code mark replaces explicit name/address, the reference to 'packer and/or dispatcher' (or equivalent) must be indicated in close connection with the code per national legislation.
Contaminants: the produce must comply with maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and with Codex pesticide maximum residue limits.

Hygiene: preparation and handling are recommended to follow the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53-2003) and other relevant Codex texts, and produce should meet any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21-1997.
